Title: The F-box protein CHALK10 mediates SEMIDWARF-1 ubiquitination and degradation to negatively regulate grain chalkiness in rice

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: F-box protein of the FBL subfamily; contains an F-box domain, 11 leucine-rich repeat (LRR) domains, and one LRR_CC domain
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Endosperm of developing and mature grains
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major grain storage carbohydrate determining grain quality, cooking properties, and endosperm appearance
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Negatively regulates grain chalkiness; promotes starch accumulation and amylose content while reducing metabolizable sugar accumulation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS mutant identification; MutMap mapping; complementation under ACTIN promoter; CRISPR-Cas9 knockout; Y2H; GST pull-down; luciferase complementation; BiFC; coIP; cell-free degradation assay; in vitro and in vivo ubiquitination assays; metabolite and starch property profiling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHALK10 was identified as the causal gene of the chalk10 mutant and is required to suppress grain chalkiness. 2. Loss of CHALK10 decreased total starch and amylose, altered starch granule packing and starch physicochemical properties, and increased soluble/metabolizable sugars. 3. CHALK10 interacts with SD1 and SCF components and promotes SD1 ubiquitination and 26S proteasome-dependent degradation. 4. The CHALK10-SD1-GA module modulates expression of starch degradation and biosynthesis genes in endosperm.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Constitutively expressed in all tissues tested, with higher expression in leaves, sheaths, and developing grains
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Developing grains/endosperm, especially during grain filling; transcripts detected in developing grains, particularly embryo
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: ACTIN promoter (complementation, constitutive); 35S promoter (subcellular localization and interaction assays)
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown plants in Hangzhou and Lingshui; grain development; genetic background effects
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: chalk10 mutation also altered agronomic traits including plant height, grain length, grain thickness, thousand-grain weight, tiller number, seed-setting rate, yield per plant, and slowed grain filling
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for improving rice grain appearance quality by reducing chalkiness; maintaining or enhancing CHALK10 activity could lower chalkiness and stabilize starch accumulation, but possible pleiotropic effects on agronomic traits should be considered
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: MutMap whole-genome sequencing of parents and F2 pools; accession number NA
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts as a substrate receptor in an SCF E3 ligase complex; interaction with SD1 was mediated by the F-box domain; discussion notes autophagy inhibitor 3-methyladenine also delayed SD1 degradation, suggesting possible crosstalk with autophagy

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream to downstream biosynthetic step supplying precursors for active GAs
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: GA pathway intermediates such as GA12/GA53-derived precursors
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: GA20 intermediates leading to active GA1, GA3, GA4, and GA7 accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes GA 20-oxidation steps in gibberellin biosynthesis, thereby promoting production of bioactive GAs
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool and promotes accumulation of active GAs that drive starch degradation and chalkiness
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step in GA biosynthesis; established in paper as decisive for chalkiness promotion via GA accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Storage carbohydrate affecting grain quality and cooking properties
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positively regulates grain chalkiness; decreases starch and amylose contents and promotes soluble sugar accumulation via elevated GA levels
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Y2H; GST pull-down; luciferase complementation; BiFC; coIP; overexpression; CRISPR-Cas9 loss-of-function mutant; genetic suppression in chalk10 sd1 double mutant; GA quantification; starch and sugar profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SD1 physically interacts with CHALK10 and is ubiquitinated/degraded by the CHALK10-containing SCF complex. 2. SD1 overexpression increased chalky grain rate and chalkiness degree and reduced total starch and amylose. 3. sd1 loss of function reduced chalkiness degree and partially/completely suppressed chalk10-associated defects. 4. SD1 promotes active GA accumulation, altered starch metabolic gene expression, increased alpha-amylase activity, and sugar accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Constitutively expressed; abundant in rice grains; transcripts accumulate in developing grains, particularly embryo
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: ACTIN promoter (overexpression, constitutive); 35S promoter (protein interaction and ubiquitination assays)
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: GA3 treatment of panicles after fertilization under natural conditions; grain development stage; endosperm at 20 DAF
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function causes shorter plants (semidwarf phenotype); overexpression increases chalkiness and worsens grain appearance quality
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reducing SD1 activity in quality-focused breeding may decrease chalkiness, but pleiotropic plant height effects must be balanced against agronomic goals; quality/yield trait coupling is a key consideration
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Known Green Revolution gene; here shown to also affect grain quality through GA-mediated control of starch metabolism
